questions.question_html: The number of diagonals of a pentagon is

options.explanation:
The diagonals of any polygon can be calculated by the formula {tex}\frac{n\times\left(n-3\right)}2{/tex} where "n" is the number of sides.
for a pentagon n = 5
{tex}\frac{5\times\left(5-3\right)}2{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{5\times\left(2\right)}2{/tex} 
5
Therefore a pentagon has 5 diagonals.

questions.question_html: The number of diagonal in a triangle are

options.explanation: Every polygon has a number of diagonals except for the triangle. A diagonal is a line that connects two non-adjacent corners together. In a triangle every corner is adjacent.

questions.question_html: A polygon of six sides is called a ________.
A triangle with all its sides of unequal lengths is called a ________ triangle
Two non-parallel line segments will always intersect.
All equilateral triangles are isosceles also.
Is ABCD given in Figure a polygon? If yes, what is the special name for it?
questions.solution_html: Yes, it is a polygon, because it is a simple closed figure made of line segments only. It is a quadrilateral.

questions.question_html:
In the figure, BCDE is a square and a 3D shape has been formed by joining the point A in space with the vertices B, C, D and E. Name the 3D shape and also its

  1. vertices
  2. edges
  3. faces
questions.solution_html:
The 3D shape formed is a square pyramid.

  1. Vertices are A, B, C, D and E.
  2. Edges are AB, AC, AD, AE, BC, CD, DE, and EB.
  3. Faces are square BCDE and triangles ABC, ACD, ADE, and ABE.

questions.question_html: The number of diagonals in a septagon is

options.explanation:
since, the number of diagonals in n(n - 3) a polygon = {tex}\frac{n(n-3)}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\because{/tex} Septagon has 7 sides, i.e., n = 7
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The number of diagonals in a septagon
= {tex}\frac{7(7-3)}{2}=\frac{7 \times 4}{2}{/tex} = 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 14

questions.question_html: The number of angles in the given figure is

options.explanation:
The names of angles formed in the given figure are {tex}\angle A O B{/tex}, {tex}\angle A O C{/tex}, {tex}\angle A O D{/tex} {tex}\angle B O C{/tex}, {tex}\angle B O D{/tex} and {tex}\angle C O D{/tex}
There are total 6 angles formed.

questions.question_html: The number of obtuse angles in Figure is

options.explanation:
The obtuse angles formed in the
given figure are
{tex}\angle A O D{/tex} = 20o + 45o + 65o = 130o
{tex}\angle B O D{/tex}  = 45o + 65o = 110o, {tex}\angle C O E{/tex} = 65o + 30o = 95o and {tex}\angle B O E{/tex} = 45o + 65o + 30o = 140o
Thus, there are total 4 obtuse angles formed.

questions.solution_html: O and S; T and N ; P, Q, R and M

questions.question_html: The number of straight angles in the given figure is ________.
The number of right angles in a straight angle is ________ and that in a complete angle is ________.
Fill in the blank: The number of common points in the two angles marked in the given figure is ________.
questions.solution_html:
Two: The two angles marked; {tex}\angle PAQ {/tex} and {tex}\angle PDQ{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The number of common points is 2 and these are P and Q.

questions.question_html: Fill in the blank: The number of common points in the two angles marked in the figure is _______.
questions.solution_html:
One 
The two angles marked; {tex} \angle C A B{/tex} and {tex}\angle D A E{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The number of common point is 1 and that is A.

questions.question_html: The number of common points in the two angles marked in figure ________.
The number of common points in the two angles marked in figure is ________.
Fill in the blank: The common part between the two angles BAC and DAB in the figure is ________.
questions.solution_html: Ray {tex}\overline{A B}{/tex}

questions.question_html: A horizontal line and a vertical line always intersect at right angles.
If line PQ || line m, then line segment PQ || m.
Two parallel lines meet each other at some point.

options.explanation:
False
Two lines in a plane which do not meet even when produced indefinitely in either direction, are known as parallel lines.

questions.question_html: Two line segments may intersect at two points.

options.explanation:
False
The intersecting point of any two line segments is only one.

questions.question_html: Many lines can pass through two given points.

options.explanation:
False
{tex}\because{/tex} There is only one line which passes through two given points.

questions.question_html: Only one line can pass through a given point.

options.explanation:
False
{tex}\because{/tex} There are infinite number of lines which passes through a given point.

questions.question_html: Two angles can have exactly five points in common.

options.explanation:
False
{tex}\because{/tex} It can have any number of points.

questions.solution_html: The line segments are AB, BC, CD, DE, AC, AD, AE, BD, BE and CE.

questions.question_html:
Name the following angles of fig. using three letters:

  1. {tex}\angle1{/tex}
  2. {tex}\angle2{/tex}
  3. {tex}\angle3{/tex}
  4. {tex}\angle 1 + \angle 2{/tex}
  5. {tex}\angle 2 + \angle 3{/tex}
  6. {tex}\angle 1 + \angle 2 + \angle 3{/tex}
  7. {tex}\angle CBA - \angle 1{/tex}
questions.solution_html:
1. {tex}\angle 1=\angle C B D{/tex}
  2. {tex}\angle 2=\angle D B E{/tex}
  3. {tex}\angle 3=\angle E B A{/tex}
  4. {tex}\angle 1 + \angle 2 = \angle DBC + \angle EBD{/tex}
  5. {tex}\angle 2 + \angle 3 = \angle EBD + \angle ABE{/tex}
  6. {tex}\angle 1 + \angle 2 + \angle 3 = \angle DBC + \angle EBD + \angle ABE{/tex}
  7. {tex}\angle CBA - \angle 1 = \angle CBA-\angle DBC{/tex}

questions.question_html:
Look at Figure. Mark a point

  1. A which is in the interior of both {tex}\angle {/tex}1 and {tex}\angle {/tex}2.
  2. B which is in the interior of only {tex}\angle {/tex}1.
  3. Point C in the interior of {tex}\angle {/tex}1.

Now, state whether points B and C lie in the interior of {tex}\angle {/tex}2 also.
questions.solution_html: Yes, the given figure shows that points B and C lie in the interior of {tex}\angle 2{/tex} also.

questions.question_html:
Find out the incorrect statement, if any, in the following: An angle is formed when we have

  1. two rays with a common end-point
  2. two line segments with a common end-point
  3. a ray and a line segment with a common end-point
questions.solution_html:
Statements ii and iii are incorrect.
{tex}\because{/tex} An angle is a figure formed by two rays sharing a common endpoint.

questions.question_html:
What is common in the following figures (i) and (ii).

Is Fig (i) that of a triangle? if not, why?
questions.solution_html:
Both the figures (i) and (ii) have 3 line segments.
No, Fig. (i) is not a triangle since the three line segments do not form a closed figure.

questions.question_html: If two rays intersect, will their point of intersection be the vertex of an angle of which the rays are the two sides?
questions.solution_html: Yes, If two rays intersect, their point of intersection will be the vertex of an angle of which the rays are the two sides.

  2. Obtuse angles: {tex}\angle B C D{/tex} and {tex}\angle B A D{/tex}.

questions.question_html:
Write the name of

  1. vertices
  2. edges and
  3. faces of the prism shown in the figure.
questions.solution_html:
Vertices: A, B, C, D, E and F
Edges: AB, BC, AC, DF, FC, BD, EF, ED and AE
Faces: EACF, EDBA, ABC, DEF and DBCF.

questions.question_html: How many edges, faces, and vertices are there in a sphere?
questions.solution_html: In a sphere, edges - 0, faces - 0 and vertices - 0.

questions.question_html: Draw all the diagonals of a pentagon ABCDE and name them.
questions.solution_html: The diagonals of a pentagon ABCDE are AC, AD, BE, BD and EC.
